product.name: Verbatim
product.tagline: A.I. JUST GOT A BULLSHIT METER.
product.description: Chrome extension that pressure-tests AI responses across multiple models via multi-model debate.

primaryHook.text: You don't know what you're missing. Find out before it costs you.

secondaryHook.text: You don't always have someone to push back. AI never does.

bodyCopyOptions: You read the AI's answer. It sounds right. Then a second voice in your head asks if it actually is. Verbatim is the second voice, on demand, before the deliverable goes out.
The AI sounds confident. You aren't, not all the way. One click sends the answer to three other models and shows you exactly where they disagree.
Your reputation is the deliverable. Verbatim pressure-tests every AI conclusion against other models, in one click, before you send it.
Confidence is not correctness. Verbatim is the difference, in one click, without leaving the page.

visualTreatments.name: Clean editorial
visualTreatments.description: White background, single hero quote in large editorial type, Verbatim pink accent, small wordmark.

visualTreatments.name: Bold all-type
visualTreatments.description: Hook text fills the frame, no imagery, high contrast, black on white or white on pink.

visualTreatments.name: Contrarian
visualTreatments.description: Red, black, and white. Aggressive. BULLSHIT METER framing pushed to the front.

visualTreatments.name: Minimal
visualTreatments.description: Lots of whitespace, small wordmark, sub-hook in lowercase, no decoration.

visualTreatments.name: Screenshot mock
visualTreatments.description: Mocked product screenshot of the Debate panel surfacing a real model disagreement.

brand.voice: Direct, adult-to-adult, slightly contrarian. No em dashes. No 'leverage', 'transform', 'revolutionize', or 'AI-powered'.

audiencePainPoints: Consultants risk reputational damage when client-facing work includes a confident but wrong AI conclusion.
Solo and boutique consultants often do not have a colleague available to challenge assumptions before delivery.
The pain is not generic hallucination risk. It is missing something important in a paid client deliverable.
competitorPatterns: Most AI-tool ads emphasize speed and productivity.
Fact-checking tools often frame the problem as generic accuracy rather than professional risk.
Consultant-facing software often uses calm editorial layouts and direct risk language.
resonantCopyPatterns: Lead with the cost of missing something, not feature mechanics.
Use a direct second-person claim that maps to client-facing anxiety.
Keep the body copy concrete: answer, models, disagreement, deliverable.
resonantVisualPatterns: High-contrast type that can be read at thumbnail size.
Minimal brand system with Verbatim pink as an accent or full-field background.
Product-like disagreement panels only when they clarify the Debate behavior.
objectionsToAddress: I already use ChatGPT or Claude.
I can manually ask another model myself.
This sounds like a nice-to-have unless it saves me from client risk.
sourceNotes: Research fields are placeholders for future scraped or human-curated evidence.
No external research API has been called for this skeleton.

referenceExamples.name: Editorial Risk Statement
referenceExamples.whyItWorks: It makes professional risk legible before explaining the product.
referenceExamples.copyPattern: One sharp risk claim followed by a concrete before-you-send-it payoff.
referenceExamples.visualPattern: White field, oversized black type, small pink accent, restrained wordmark.
referenceExamples.caution: Do not make it feel like generic fear marketing.

referenceExamples.name: Contrarian Meter
referenceExamples.whyItWorks: It carries the BULLSHIT METER energy without needing a long explanation.
referenceExamples.copyPattern: Short skeptical headline with a blunt proof or consequence line.
referenceExamples.visualPattern: Pink, black, and white. Dense headline. Minimal secondary text.
referenceExamples.caution: Use the edge sparingly so it does not become a bumper sticker.

referenceExamples.name: Model Disagreement Proof
referenceExamples.whyItWorks: It shows the practical value: other models challenge what the first model missed.
referenceExamples.copyPattern: State the uncertainty, then show that Verbatim finds disagreement in one click.
referenceExamples.visualPattern: Simplified product panel with model disagreement rows and a clear verdict area.
referenceExamples.caution: Do not fake precision or imply automated truth certification.

templateCandidates.layoutPattern: Large headline top-left, concise body copy below, thin Verbatim pink rule, small wordmark footer.
templateCandidates.copyPattern: Risk-first headline plus before-the-deliverable body copy.
visualRules: Use white or light pink background.
Keep headline readable at thumbnail size.
Use one Verbatim pink accent only.
templateCandidates.whyThisTemplate: It is the cleanest first template for consultant pain because it emphasizes reputational risk without visual noise.

templateCandidates.layoutPattern: Full-frame type block, pink or black background, brand line used as a small supporting element.
templateCandidates.copyPattern: Blunt skeptical hook with short consequence-driven body copy.
visualRules: Limit palette to pink, black, and white.
Keep the brand line secondary to the actual hook.
templateCandidates.whyThisTemplate: It tests Verbatim's sharper voice while staying tied to the consultant risk wedge.

templateCandidates.layoutPattern: Hook above a simplified Debate panel showing model disagreement and a concise takeaway.
templateCandidates.copyPattern: Uncertainty claim followed by proof that multiple models can surface disagreement.
visualRules: Use product-like UI only as a mock until real screenshots are approved.
Label the panel as an example if rendered later.
Do not imply Verbatim guarantees correctness.
templateCandidates.whyThisTemplate: It may help users understand the Debate behavior, but it needs real product screenshot validation before image generation.
